impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the unpatented products qualify as 'nonstaple goods' lacking substantial non-infringing uses that would shield the conduct from antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its '844 patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the district court's determination that whether the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses was a jury question, and affirmed the resulting damages award. This case reinforces that patent holders face significant antitrust exposure when leveraging patent rights to compel purchases of unpatented products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors like BASF benefit from clear precedent that patent holders cannot use licensing leverage to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets, opening up market access.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face heightened antitrust risk when structuring exclusive supply arrangements tied to patent licenses; the 'nonstaple goods' defense is a fact-intensive jury question, creating litigation uncertainty.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was appropriate on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on the same allegedly fraudulent patent procurement.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both Tenaris's inequitable conduct and Global Tubing's Walker Process fraud claim, finding genuine disputes of material fact precluded resolution at summary judgment on both issues. The case involves allegations that Tenaris failed to disclose prior art (the CYMAX Documents) during prosecution of patents related to coiled tubing products. This decision ensures both inequitable conduct defenses and Walker Process antitrust counterclaims will proceed to trial, raising the stakes for patent applicants who fail to disclose material prior art.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers benefit from the court's willingness to let Walker Process fraud and inequitable conduct claims survive summary judgment, strengthening leverage against potentially fraudulently procured patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased exposure to both unenforceability and antitrust damages when prior art disclosure practices are questioned, as courts will allow fact-intensive inquiries to proceed to trial.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions by emulating programs and comparing function calls to a model are directed to abstract ideas under Alice step one, and whether damages can be awarded for foreign sales of infringing software.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million jury verdict, holding that Columbia's cybersecurity patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program behavior through emulation and comparison to function-call models are abstract at Alice step one, requiring remand for step two analysis. The court also held the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales. This decision significantly impacts patent eligibility for software-based cybersecurity inventions and limits extraterritorial damages in software patent cases.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from narrowed patent eligibility for method-of-detection software patents and clearer limits on extraterritorial patent damages, reducing exposure to large infringement verdicts.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the cybersecurity and software detection space face greater difficulty establishing patent eligibility, and cannot recover damages based on foreign sales even for globally distributed software products.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-based methods and systems for tailoring content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a jury verdict of infringement in favor of GoTV, holding on Netflix's cross-appeal that all asserted claims across three related patents covering server-based content adaptation for wireless devices are patent-ineligible under § 101. The court entered judgment for Netflix. This ruling has broad implications for patents covering adaptive content delivery and responsive design technologies used throughout the streaming and mobile content industries.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming services and content delivery platforms benefit from reduced patent exposure for adaptive bitrate streaming and device-responsive content delivery technologies.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with portfolios covering content adaptation, responsive design, and server-side optimization for mobile devices face heightened invalidity risk under § 101.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent that missed the deadline for a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) in an original declaratory judgment action may obtain a mandatory stay by voluntarily dismissing and refiling the same action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not circumvent the statutory deadline for obtaining a mandatory stay of a parallel district court action by dismissing and refiling its declaratory judgment action after missing the 30-day window in the original action. The court found this would render the statutory time limits meaningless. This decision is significant for pharmaceutical and technology companies managing parallel ITC investigations and district court patent litigation.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers (like NDA filers) benefit from assurance that their district court actions, including safe harbor defenses, cannot be indefinitely stayed through procedural gamesmanship by ITC respondents.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the § 1659(a)(2) stay deadline cannot cure the deficiency by refiling, requiring more rigorous docket management in parallel patent proceedings.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated Section 337 by infringing Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing and selling Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen functionality covered by Masimo's patents, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation. The court found no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings. This high-profile decision has major commercial implications for Apple's wearable device business and reinforces the ITC's role as a powerful enforcement mechanism for patent holders.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in wearable health technology are affirmed in using ITC proceedings to obtain exclusion orders against major tech companies importing infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies integrating patented health-monitoring features into consumer devices face significant import exclusion risk, potentially requiring design-arounds or licensing to maintain market access.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent holder's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a damages remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft that was based on the absence of a quantified remedy. The court found the exclusion was an abuse of discretion, and also vacated the denial of Exafer's motion to reopen discovery. This decision is important for patent litigation practice as it limits courts' ability to dispose of infringement cases solely by excluding damages evidence.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from greater protection against dispositive exclusion of damages evidence, preserving their ability to pursue infringement claims to trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Microsoft lose a potential pathway to summary judgment based on Daubert exclusion of damages experts, potentially increasing litigation costs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denial of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding the Board's exercise of discretionary authority to decline IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains authority over non-institution decisions even after Board delegation. This preserves the PTO's flexibility in managing IPR institution decisions without formal rulemaking.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's continued ability to decline IPR petitions under flexible policy guidance, reducing the risk of post-grant challenges to their patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and frequent IPR petitioners like Apple face continued uncertainty from PTO discretionary denials with limited ability to challenge the institutional framework through APA challenges.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed the claim term 'encryption information' in a patent covering systems for streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the prior art rendered the claims obvious under the correct construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's streaming media patent and vacated the Board's decision upholding the patent's validity, remanding for reconsideration of Netflix's obviousness challenge under the corrected construction. This decision impacts the streaming technology space by potentially invalidating patent claims covering partial encryption of streamed media content.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming services benefit from the potential invalidation of patents covering encrypted media streaming technologies, reducing licensing costs and litigation risk.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the DRM and content protection space face risk that narrower claim constructions will expose their patents to obviousness challenges on remand.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims containing a means-plus-function 'control means' limitation in a crop harvester patent are indefinite when the specification describes corresponding structure using terms like 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that asserted claims of a crop harvester header-height patent were invalid as indefinite, holding that the specification adequately disclosed corresponding structure for the means-plus-function 'control means' limitation. This decision provides important guidance on the level of structural disclosure required in specifications to support means-plus-function claims, particularly when conventional components are referenced.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from a more permissive standard for disclosing corresponding structure in means-plus-function claims, particularly for well-known conventional components.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Deere face revived infringement claims after losing an indefiniteness defense, with the case remanded for further proceedings.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute created a money-mandating obligation enforceable under the Tucker Act when the SBA failed to award grants to eligible applicants in the order applications were received.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of Federal Claims' denial of the government's motion to dismiss, holding that over 300 restaurants stated viable Tucker Act claims for unpaid RRF grants. The court found that 15 U.S.C. § 9009c's use of 'shall award grants' and 'shall' award them 'in the order in which applications are received' created a money-mandating obligation. This decision allows hundreds of small businesses to proceed with claims that the SBA improperly prioritized grant applications, potentially resulting in significant government liability.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses that applied for but did not receive RRF grants may recover damages, establishing that statutory grant programs using mandatory language create enforceable payment obligations.
impacts.negativeImplications: The federal government faces potential liability for grant administration decisions, as mandatory statutory language in appropriations programs may create judicially enforceable payment obligations.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations, and whether incentive awards to class representatives were proper.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the $125 million settlement of a nationwide class action challenging the federal judiciary's use of PACER fees to fund non-PACER expenses, including CM/ECF systems and courtroom technology. The court upheld the settlement, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to nonprofit class representatives, rejecting an objector's challenges. This decision validates that PACER fees were unlawfully used beyond statutory authority and results in reimbursement to hundreds of thousands of PACER users.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses, law firms, and other frequent PACER users will receive reimbursement for overcharged fees, and the decision may constrain future government fee-setting for electronic court records access.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute, and whether the common law right of public access to judicial records applies to such materials.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the CIT's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in trade remedy investigations is incompatible with the statute and does not override the common law right of access to judicial records. The court treated the ITC's mandamus petition as an appeal under the collateral order doctrine. This decision significantly impacts trade remedy proceedings by requiring the ITC to make individualized confidentiality determinations rather than applying automatic blanket designations.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries, trade associations, and the public gain greater transparency into ITC trade remedy proceedings, enabling more informed participation and oversight of antidumping and countervailing duty investigations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign manufacturers and exporters that submit questionnaire responses in ITC investigations face potential public disclosure of business information previously treated as automatically confidential, increasing compliance burdens.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether IHS registered nurses hired under Title 5 but paid under Title 38 base pay provisions are entitled to enhanced compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38 and Title 5 regulations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the dismissal of claims by IHS nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work during the COVID-19 pandemic, finding the nurses stated valid claims under both 38 U.S.C. § 7453 and Title 5 pay regulations. The court held that IHS's failure to apply available Title 38 enhanced compensation provisions to Title 5 healthcare employees was actionable. This decision could result in significant backpay liability for the government and affects compensation practices for federal healthcare workers.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal healthcare workers may recover backpay for enhanced compensation, establishing clearer entitlements under the hybrid Title 5/Title 38 pay framework.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal agencies employing healthcare workers under hybrid pay frameworks face potential class-wide backpay liability and must reassess compensation practices for overtime, nighttime, holiday, and Sunday work.

impacts.caseName: Fortress Iron, LP v. Digger Specialties, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents are invalid for omission of a coinventor when the patent holder cannot locate the omitted coinventor to obtain consent for correction under 35 U.S.C. § 256, and whether the court may order correction under § 256(b) without consent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity for two patents due to omission of a coinventor whose contributions were acknowledged but who could not be located for consent-based correction. The court also affirmed denial of Fortress's motion for judicial correction under § 256(b). This decision highlights the critical importance of properly identifying and maintaining contact with all coinventors, as failure to do so can result in complete patent invalidity with no available remedy.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers gain a powerful invalidity defense when patent holders fail to properly name coinventors, particularly where foreign collaborators contributed to the invention.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies relying on collaborative product development with foreign partners face significant patent invalidity risk if coinventors are not properly identified and their contact maintained throughout the patent lifecycle.
